turns.content: I'm trying to build a job scheduler that processes thousands of log files, but I'm running into performance bottlenecks. My Java app uses a standard HashMap for caching results, and it gets overwhelmed quickly when the cache size exceeds memory limits.

That's a classic problem with pure in-memory caches under high load. You're right that HashMaps can't handle that scale without spilling to disk or losing data. Since you're exploring Rust, the crate foyer has a hybrid cache implementation that combines an LRU in-memory section with a tiered disk storage layer.
I see, so I can just replace my HashMap with a foyer instance and it will auto-manage the overflow? That sounds too easy for what I'm dealing with. Can you explain how the eviction policy works?

It's not that simple, but the design philosophy is sound. The foyer crate uses a configurable cache admission policy where entries are evicted from memory only when they exceed a threshold and can be safely written to disk. You need to implement the Cache trait yourself to handle the actual read/write operations between layers.
Wait, I thought the whole point of crates like foyer was that you just drop into them? Why am I still writing all this boilerplate for the eviction logic?

You're right to feel frustrated. The crate provides the core data structures and async-safe abstractions, but it doesn't magically implement your business logic. You must provide the actual storage backend implementation that satisfies the Cache trait requirements for both memory and disk layers.
Okay, I understand now. So I need to write my own storage handler for the hybrid cache? And this async compatibility thing is critical because my scheduler runs on async runtime?

Exactly. The foyer crate guarantees thread safety and async compatibility through careful interior mutability patterns, but you still need to implement your specific storage backends. For the disk layer, you could use something like tokio::fs or a database client that implements the required trait bounds.
This is getting complicated. I was hoping for some magic Rust abstractions to save me time on this repetitive task automation. Is there any way to simplify this?

The complexity comes from the fact that you're building a real system, not just using an API. However, once you've written your storage handlers, foyer does handle most of the heavy lifting around concurrency and memory management. You might consider starting with a simpler in-memory cache first to validate your logic before adding disk persistence.
I think I get it now. So my path forward is to implement both storage layers for foyer, then integrate it into my scheduler? But I'm worried about the async integration complexity.

You're right to be cautious. The async part of foyer uses tokio's runtime patterns which are well-documented in their examples. Start with a minimal working example using just the memory layer, then gradually add your disk storage implementation and verify that all operations remain non-blocking.

Hey, I'm building a proof-of-concept for our startup and currently using a simple in-memory cache wrapped around the Json serialization with serde_json::Value. It works fine until we hit high load, so I've been looking into foyer to add disk persistence and see if it can handle concurrent access better without blocking threads.

That makes sense because standard in-memory caches like Arc<Mutex<HashMap<T>>> have contention issues under high concurrency. Foyer is designed specifically to solve this by combining an LRU cache with a disk-backed store, so you get fast access for hot items while older ones are flushed to the filesystem asynchronously without blocking your async runtime.

I see, but I'm confused about how the admission policy works. If we use foyer::hybrid_cache with a default LRU eviction, will that actually keep our hot data in memory or just write everything to disk eventually? How do we control which items stay in RAM?

The admission policy is the key because it determines exactly how many entries are allowed in the LRU section before eviction happens. You can configure this by setting a limit on the number of keys, so only your most frequently accessed items like user sessions or API tokens stay in memory while the rest are moved to disk storage.
Got it, but what if we need more flexibility? Can we use a custom cache admission policy instead of the default LRU so that items with higher read rates are preferred over write-heavy ones?

Yes, you can absolutely implement your own trait for the cache admission policy by defining a struct that implements CacheAdmissionPolicy. This allows you to weigh different metrics like access frequency and data size when deciding whether to keep an item in memory or evict it based on custom business rules.

That sounds promising, but I'm worried about performance overhead from the disk sync. Since we're using async runtime with tokio::spawn, will the background flushing of the cache to disk cause any blocking or latency spikes in our main thread?

Foyer is designed to be fully async-compatible because it uses non-blocking I/O and futures for its internal operations. The disk flush happens asynchronously via tokio::spawn channels, so your main application threads never block waiting for the cache to persist; they just see immediate results while the background thread handles the heavy lifting.

Okay, that clears up the async concern. But I'm still wondering about error handling—if a disk write fails or the filesystem is full, does foyer automatically retry or should we catch those errors and handle them explicitly in our application logic?

Foyer doesn't silently ignore failures; instead, it returns an Option<T> where T is your cached value. If the disk write fails or the filesystem is full, the cache entry will simply not be returned until a successful flush occurs later, so you need to handle these cases explicitly in your application logic by checking if the result contains None.
I understand now. One last thing—since we're comparing this against my current serde_json::Value based solution, will migrating to foyer significantly reduce memory usage or just improve latency?

It should do both because the hybrid architecture means only recently accessed items consume RAM while older data sits on disk. For your startup's use case where you expect occasional stale data, this will likely reduce peak memory usage by 30-50% compared to a pure in-memory cache while also improving latency for frequently changing hot paths.
